q:
Two blocks of masses 3 kg and 5 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley. The breaking stress of the metal is 24π×102 N m-2. What is the minimum radius of the wire ?
( take g=10 m s-2
question:
Two blocks of masses 3 kg and 5 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley. The breaking stress of the metal is 24π×102 N m-2. What is the minimum radius of the wire ?
( take g=10 m s-2
solution:
Tension in the string =2m1m2m1+m2g

=2×3×5×108=752

Stress =TA

24π×102=752πr2

r2=7548×10-2

r2=0.015625

r=0.125 m=12.5 cm

subject: Physics
q: A force is applied to a steel wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, rigidly clamped at one end. As a result elongation in the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. If same force is applied to another steel wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of double the length and a diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math> times that of the wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, the elongation in the wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be (wires having uniform circular cross sections)
question: A force is applied to a steel wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, rigidly clamped at one end. As a result elongation in the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. If same force is applied to another steel wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of double the length and a diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math> times that of the wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, the elongation in the wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be (wires having uniform circular cross sections)
solution:
As we know, Y=FA∆ll

⇒F=YAl∆l

For both cases force applied is the same, therefore

YA∆ll1=YA∆ll2

⇒∆l2∆l1=A1A2×l2l1

⇒∆l20.2=πR2π2.4R2×21

⇒∆l2=6.9×10-2 mm

subject: Physics
q: An aluminium rod with Young’s modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> undergoes elastic strain of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The energy per unit volume stored in the rod in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>SI</mi></math> unit
question: An aluminium rod with Young’s modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> undergoes elastic strain of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The energy per unit volume stored in the rod in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>SI</mi></math> unit
solution:
The data given is 

Y=7×1010 N m-2 and∆ll=0.04100

Young's modulus is given by the ratio of stress and strain,

Y=FA∆ll=FlA∆l

The energy stored is given as 

E=YA2l∆l2⇒E=YA2∆ll2×l   ...(i)

The energy per unit volume can be written from equation (i)

EV=Y2×∆ll2   ...(ii)

Substituting the values in equation (ii)

EV=12×7×1010×0.04×0.04104=56×102

subject: Physics
q: A string $A$ of length $0.314$ m and Young's modulus $2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$ is connected to another string $B$ of length and Young's modulus both twice of those of $A$. This series combination of strings is then suspended from a rigid support and its free end is fixed to a load of mass $0.8$ kg. The net change in length of the combination is _____ mm. (radius of both the strings is $0.2$ mm and acceleration due to gravity $= 10$ m/s$^2$) (Mass of both strings is to be neglected as compared to the mass of load)
question: A string $A$ of length $0.314$ m and Young's modulus $2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$ is connected to another string $B$ of length and Young's modulus both twice of those of $A$. This series combination of strings is then suspended from a rigid support and its free end is fixed to a load of mass $0.8$ kg. The net change in length of the combination is _____ mm. (radius of both the strings is $0.2$ mm and acceleration due to gravity $= 10$ m/s$^2$) (Mass of both strings is to be neglected as compared to the mass of load)
solution:
Given:
Length of string A, $L_A = 0.314$ m
Young's modulus of string A, $Y_A = 2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$
Radius of both strings, $r = 0.2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m
Length of string B, $L_B = 2L_A = 0.628$ m
Young's modulus of string B, $Y_B = 2Y_A = 4 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$
Mass of load, $m = 0.8$ kg
Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10$ m/s$^2$

Tension in both strings, $T = mg = 0.8 \times 10 = 8$ N

Cross-sectional area of both strings:
$A = \pi r^2 = 3.14 \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 3.14 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m$^2$

Elongation in string A:
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{T L_A}{A Y_A}$
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{8 \times 0.314}{3.14 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 10^{10}}$
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{8 \times 0.314}{8 \times 3.14 \times 10^2} = \dfrac{0.1}{10^2} = 10^{-3}$ m $= 1$ mm

Elongation in string B:
$\Delta L_B = \dfrac{T L_B}{A Y_B}$
$\Delta L_B = \dfrac{8 \times (2 \times 0.314)}{A \times (2 Y_A)} = \dfrac{T L_A}{A Y_A} = 1$ mm

Net change in length of the combination:
$\Delta L = \Delta L_A + \Delta L_B = 1 + 1 = 2$ mm

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q: The length of a wire becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> tension are applied respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>11</mn><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, then the natural length of wire will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. Here the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: The length of a wire becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> tension are applied respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>11</mn><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, then the natural length of wire will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. Here the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
Young's modulus is given by Y=FA∆ll

⇒FA=YΔll⇒Δl=FlAY

⇒l+Δl=l1⇒F1LAY+L=LF1AY+1=l1   ...(i)

Similarly,

l2=F2AY+1L

⇒10l2=11l1

It is given that 10l1=11l2

⇒10×LF2AY+1=11LF1AY+1

⇒10F2AY+10=11F1AY+11

Substituting F1=100 N, F2=120 N

⇒1200AY−1100AY=1⇒100AY=1

⇒1AY=1100

Using equation (i),

 L=l1F1AY+1=l1100100+1=l12

subject: Physics
q: The two wires $A$ and $B$ of equal cross-section but of different materials are joined together. The ratio of Young's modulus of wire $A$ and wire $B$ is $20/11$. When the joined wire is kept under certain tension the elongations in the wires $A$ and $B$ are equal. If the length of wire $A$ is $2.2$ m, then the length of wire $B$ is _______ m.
question: The two wires $A$ and $B$ of equal cross-section but of different materials are joined together. The ratio of Young's modulus of wire $A$ and wire $B$ is $20/11$. When the joined wire is kept under certain tension the elongations in the wires $A$ and $B$ are equal. If the length of wire $A$ is $2.2$ m, then the length of wire $B$ is _______ m.
solution:
Given that the wires have equal cross-sectional area $A$ and are under the same tension $F$, the elongation $\Delta L$ is given by:

$\Delta L = \dfrac{F L}{A Y}$

Since the elongations in both wires are equal ($\Delta L_A = \Delta L_B$):

$\dfrac{F L_A}{A Y_A} = \dfrac{F L_B}{A Y_B}$

$\dfrac{L_A}{Y_A} = \dfrac{L_B}{Y_B}$

$L_B = L_A \times \dfrac{Y_B}{Y_A}$

Substituting the given values $L_A = 2.2$ m and $\dfrac{Y_A}{Y_B} = \dfrac{20}{11}$:

$L_B = 2.2 \times \dfrac{11}{20}$

$L_B = \dfrac{24.2}{20} = 1.21$ m

Answer: $1.21$

question: Two blocks of mass 2 kg and 4 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley as shown in figure. The radius of wire is 4.0×10-5 m and Young's modulus of the metal is 2.0×1011 N m-2. The longitudinal strain developed in the wire is 1απ. The value of α is _____. [Use g=10 m s-2)
solution:
For the given system, T=2m1m2m1+m2g=803 N

Area of the wire, A=πr2=16π×10−10 m2

Strain =Δll=FAY=TAY

=80316π×10−10×2×1011=112π

⇒α=12

subject: Physics
q: Two metallic wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> have same volume and are made up of same material. If their area of cross sections are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is applied to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, an extension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>l</mi></math> is produced. The force which is required to produce same extension in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mo> </mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is ______.
question: Two metallic wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> have same volume and are made up of same material. If their area of cross sections are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is applied to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, an extension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>l</mi></math> is produced. The force which is required to produce same extension in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mo> </mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is ______.
solution:
The formula for Young's modulus is given by

Y=FA∆ll=FlA∆l

From above equation, it follows that

Δl=FlAY   ...1

Again, volume of the wire is given by

V=Al⇒l=VA

Hence, from equation (1),

∆l=FVA2Y   ...2

As, Y and V is the same for both the wires,

∆l∝F A2

Hence, for two wires, it can be written that

∆l1∆l2=F1 A12×A22 F2   ...3

As ∆l1=∆l2, from equation (3), it follows that

F1 A22=F2 A12⇒F1 F2=A12 A22=412=16

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped at one end. If its other end is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>. If the radius of the wire and the applied force both are reduced to half of their original values keeping original length constant, the increase in length will become:
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped at one end. If its other end is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>. If the radius of the wire and the applied force both are reduced to half of their original values keeping original length constant, the increase in length will become:
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire, in the first case, can be written as

Y=Fπr2lL   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

F=Yπr2×lL   ...2

For the second case, the Young's modulus is given by

Y=F/2πr2/4l'L   ...3

From equation (3), it follows that

F=Yl'L×2×πr24   ...4

From equations (2) and (4), it implies that

Yπr2×lL=Yl'L×2×πr24⇒l'=2l

The equilibrium extended length, of this wire, would equal:
question: A uniformly tapering conical wire is made from a material of Young's modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> and has a normal, unextended length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. The radii, at the upper and lower ends of this conical wire, have values <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math>, respectively. The equilibrium extended length, of this wire, would equal:
solution:
r=2RLx+R

For a small section

FA=Ydldx

⟹∫0LdL=∫0LMgdxπ2RLx×R2Y



∆L=MgπY-12RxL+R0L×L2R

=MgL3πR2Y

L f =L+ΔL=L[ 1+ mg 3π R 2 Y ]

subject: Physics
q: A wire suspended vertically from one of its ends is stretched by attaching a weight of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ to the lower end. The weight stretches the wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the elastic energy stored in the wire is
question: A wire suspended vertically from one of its ends is stretched by attaching a weight of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ to the lower end. The weight stretches the wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the elastic energy stored in the wire is
solution:
Elastic energy $=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{x}$
$$
\mathrm{F}=200 \mathrm{~N}, \mathrm{x}=1 \mathrm{~mm}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} \quad \therefore \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}=0.1 \mathrm{~J}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Two wires A and B are made of same material having ratio of lengths $\frac{L_A}{L_B}=\frac{1}{3}$ and their diameters ratio $\frac{d_A}{d_B}=2$. If both the wires are stretched using same force, what would be the ratio of their respective elongations?
question: Two wires A and B are made of same material having ratio of lengths $\frac{L_A}{L_B}=\frac{1}{3}$ and their diameters ratio $\frac{d_A}{d_B}=2$. If both the wires are stretched using same force, what would be the ratio of their respective elongations?
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{B}}}=2$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}$
Given, $F_A=F_B$ and $Y_A=Y_B$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}}{\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}}}\right)$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\left(\frac{\frac{\pi}{4} \mathrm{~d}_{\mathrm{B}}^2}{\frac{\pi}{4} \mathrm{~d}_{\mathrm{A}}^2}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}}}\right)^2$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{12}$

question: A man grows into a giant such that his linear dimensions increase by a factor of <math><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Assuming that his density remains same, the stress in the leg will change by a factor of:
solution:
Stress =FA=MgA

=ρVolume .gA

=ρ93x3 g92x2

=9 times

subject: Physics
q: Two wires are made of the same material and have the same volume. However wire 1 has crosssectional area $A$ and wire-2 has cross-sectional area $3 A$. If the length of wire 1 increases by $\Delta x$ on applying force $F$, how much force is needed to stretch wire 2 by the same amount?
question: Two wires are made of the same material and have the same volume. However wire 1 has crosssectional area $A$ and wire-2 has cross-sectional area $3 A$. If the length of wire 1 increases by $\Delta x$ on applying force $F$, how much force is needed to stretch wire 2 by the same amount?
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}_1 \ell_1=\mathrm{A}_2 \ell_2 \Rightarrow \ell_2=\frac{\mathrm{A}_1 \ell_1}{\mathrm{~A}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{A} \times \ell_1}{3 \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{\ell_1}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}=3 \\
& \Delta \mathrm{x}_1=\frac{\mathrm{F}_1}{\mathrm{~A} \gamma} \times \ell_1 \\
& \Delta \mathrm{x}_2=\frac{\mathrm{F}_2}{3 \mathrm{~A} \gamma} \ell_2 \\
& \text { Here } \Delta \mathrm{x}_1=\Delta \mathrm{x}_2 \\
& \frac{\mathrm{F}_2}{3 \mathrm{~A} \gamma} \ell_2=\frac{\mathrm{F}_1}{\mathrm{~A} \gamma} \ell_1 \\
& \left.\mathrm{~F}_2=3 \mathrm{~F}_1 \times \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}=3 \mathrm{~F}_1 \times 3=\mathrm{i}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Two wires of same length and radius are joined end to end and loaded. The Young's moduli of the materials of the two wires are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The combination behaves as a single wire then its Young's modulus is:
question: Two wires of same length and radius are joined end to end and loaded. The Young's moduli of the materials of the two wires are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The combination behaves as a single wire then its Young's modulus is:
solution:
In series combination, Δl=ℓ1+ℓ2

Y=F/AΔℓ/ℓ⇒Δℓ=FℓAY

⇒Δℓ∝ℓY

Equivalent length of rod after joining is =2ℓ

As, lengths are same and force is also same in series

Δℓ=Δℓ1+Δℓ2

ℓeqYeq=ℓY1+ℓY2⇒2ℓY=ℓY1+ℓY2

∴ Y=2Y1Y2Y1+Y2

subject: Physics
q: A uniform wire of length $l$ of weight $w$ is suspended from the roof with a weight of $W$ at the other end. The stress in the wire at $\dfrac{l}{3}$ distance from the top is $\left(\dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{\gamma}\dfrac{w}{A}\right)$, where, $A$ is the cross sectional area of the wire. The value of $\gamma$ is _______.
question: A uniform wire of length $l$ of weight $w$ is suspended from the roof with a weight of $W$ at the other end. The stress in the wire at $\dfrac{l}{3}$ distance from the top is $\left(\dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{\gamma}\dfrac{w}{A}\right)$, where, $A$ is the cross sectional area of the wire. The value of $\gamma$ is _______.
solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the wire at a distance $\dfrac{l}{3}$ from the top.

The length of the wire below this point is $l - \dfrac{l}{3} = \dfrac{2l}{3}$.

The weight of this lower part of the wire is $\dfrac{w}{l} \times \dfrac{2l}{3} = \dfrac{2w}{3}$.

The total downward force (tension $T$) at this point is the sum of the weight $W$ attached at the bottom and the weight of the lower part of the wire:

$T = W + \dfrac{2w}{3}$

The stress at this point is given by the tension divided by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$\text{Stress} = \dfrac{T}{A} = \dfrac{W + \dfrac{2w}{3}}{A} = \dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{3}\dfrac{w}{A}$

Comparing this with the given expression for stress, $\dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{\gamma}\dfrac{w}{A}$, we get:

$\gamma = 3$

Answer: $3$

subject: Physics
q: The length of metallic wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> when tension in it is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. It is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when the tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The original length of the wire will be :
question: The length of metallic wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> when tension in it is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. It is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when the tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The original length of the wire will be :
solution:
Assuming Hooke's law to be valid

T∝Δℓ

T=kΔℓ

Let,ℓ0= natural length (original length) ⇒T=kℓ-ℓ0

so, T1=kℓ1-ℓ0 & T2=kℓ2-ℓ0

⇒T1T2=ℓ1-ℓ0ℓ2-ℓ0

⇒ℓ0=T2ℓ1-T1ℓ2 T2-T1

question: A steel wire can sustain $100 \mathrm{~kg}$ weight without breaking. If the wire is cut into two equal parts, each part can sustain a weight of

subject: Physics
q:
One end of a metal wire is fixed to a ceiling and a load of 2 kg hangs from the other end. A similar wire is attached to the bottom of the load and another load of 1 kg hangs from this lower wire. Then the ratio of longitudinal strain of upper wire to that of the lower wire will be

[Area of cross section of wire =0.005 cm2, Y=2×1011 N m-2 and g=10 m s-2]
question:
One end of a metal wire is fixed to a ceiling and a load of 2 kg hangs from the other end. A similar wire is attached to the bottom of the load and another load of 1 kg hangs from this lower wire. Then the ratio of longitudinal strain of upper wire to that of the lower wire will be

[Area of cross section of wire =0.005 cm2, Y=2×1011 N m-2 and g=10 m s-2]
solution:
As we know, change in length is given by ΔL=FLAY

Therefore, strain required ΔLL=FAY

Now the ratio will be, ΔL1 L1ΔL2 L2=F1 F2=T1 T2=3010=3

subject: Physics
q: A copper wire of length $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ and a steel wire of length $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ having equal cross-sectional areas are joined end to end. The composite wire is stretched by a certain load which stretches the copper wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. If the Young's modulii of copper and steel are respectively $1.0 \times 10^{11}$ $\mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ and $2.0 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, the total extension of the composite wire is :
question: A copper wire of length $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ and a steel wire of length $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ having equal cross-sectional areas are joined end to end. The composite wire is stretched by a certain load which stretches the copper wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. If the Young's modulii of copper and steel are respectively $1.0 \times 10^{11}$ $\mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ and $2.0 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, the total extension of the composite wire is :
solution:
$\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{c}} \times\left(\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{c}} / \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)=\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{s}} \times\left(\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}} / \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}}\right)$
$$
\Rightarrow 1 \times 10^{11} \times\left(\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1}\right)=2 \times 10^{11} \times\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}}}{0.5}\right)
$$

$$
\therefore \Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=0.25 \mathrm{~mm}
$$
Therefore, total extension of the composite
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { wire }=\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{c}}+\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}} \\
& =1 \mathrm{~mm}+0.25 \mathrm{~m}=1.25 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
The volume contraction of a solid copper cube of edge length 10 cm , when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of $7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Pa}$, would be ____ $\mathrm{mm}^3$.
(Given bulk modulus of copper $=1.4 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
question:
The volume contraction of a solid copper cube of edge length 10 cm , when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of $7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Pa}$, would be ____ $\mathrm{mm}^3$.
(Given bulk modulus of copper $=1.4 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{V}=\frac{7 \times 10^6}{1.4 \times 10^{11}} \times\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^3 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{~V}=50 \mathrm{~mm}^3\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two wires $A$ and $B$ made of different materials of lengths 6.0 cm and 5.4 cm, respectively and area of cross sections $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$, respectively are stretched by the same magnitude under a given load. The ratio of the Young's modulus of $A$ to that of $B$ is $x: 3$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Two wires $A$ and $B$ made of different materials of lengths 6.0 cm and 5.4 cm, respectively and area of cross sections $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$, respectively are stretched by the same magnitude under a given load. The ratio of the Young's modulus of $A$ to that of $B$ is $x: 3$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Young's modulus: $Y = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \frac{F/A}{\Delta L/L}$.
When the same force is applied and both wires stretch by the same extension $\Delta L$, the ratio of Young's moduli is:
$\frac{Y_A}{Y_B} = \frac{F/A_A}{\Delta L/L_A} \times \frac{\Delta L/L_B}{F/A_B} = \frac{A_B}{A_A} \times \frac{L_A}{L_B}$
$=\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} \times \frac{6.0}{5.4} = 1.5 \times \frac{10}{9} = \frac{5}{3}$.
Therefore $x : 3 = 5 : 3$, giving $x = 5$.

subject: Physics
q: Young’s moduli of the material of wires A and B are in the ratio of 1 : 4, while its area of cross sections are in the ratio of 1 : 3. If the same amount of load is applied to both the wires, the amount of elongation produced in the wires A and B will be in the ratio of [Assume length of wires A and B are same]
question: Young’s moduli of the material of wires A and B are in the ratio of 1 : 4, while its area of cross sections are in the ratio of 1 : 3. If the same amount of load is applied to both the wires, the amount of elongation produced in the wires A and B will be in the ratio of [Assume length of wires A and B are same]
solution:
The formula to calculate the elongation of a metallic wire because of the application of a load can be written as

ΔL=FLAY   ...1

The ratio of the elongations for both the wires is, then, calculated as follows-

∆L1∆L2=FLA1Y1FLA2Y2= A2Y2A1Y1=31×41=121

subject: Physics
q: If average depth of an ocean is 4000 m and the bulk modulus of water is 2×109 N m-2, then fractional compression ΔVV of water at the bottom of ocean is α×10-2. The value of α is _______, (Given, g=10 m s-2, ρ=1000 kg m-3)
question: If average depth of an ocean is 4000 m and the bulk modulus of water is 2×109 N m-2, then fractional compression ΔVV of water at the bottom of ocean is α×10-2. The value of α is _______, (Given, g=10 m s-2, ρ=1000 kg m-3)
solution:
The formula to calculate the Bulk modulus is given by

B=-∆P∆VV   ...1

From equation (1), the fractional compression in volume may be obtained as follows:

-ΔVV=∆PB=ρg∆hB   ...2

From equation (2) it follows that

-∆VV=1000×10×40002×109⇒∆VV=2×10-2

Hence, α=2.

subject: Physics
q: The strain-stress plot for materials $A, B, C$ and $D$ is shown in the figure. Which material has the largest Young's modulus ?
question: The strain-stress plot for materials $A, B, C$ and $D$ is shown in the figure. Which material has the largest Young's modulus ?
solution:
Young's modulus ($Y$) is defined as the ratio of stress to strain: $Y = \frac{\text{stress}}{\text{strain}}$.
In the given graph, the y-axis represents strain and the x-axis represents stress.
The slope of the line in a strain-stress graph is given by $m = \frac{\text{strain}}{\text{stress}}$.
Therefore, $Y = \frac{1}{\text{slope}}$.
To have the largest Young's modulus, the material must have the smallest slope in the strain-stress plot.
From the figure, line $C$ has the smallest angle with the stress axis (x-axis), meaning it has the minimum slope.
Since $Y \propto \frac{1}{\text{slope}}$, material $C$ has the largest Young's modulus.

subject: Physics
q:
In an experiment, brass and steel wires of length 1 m each with areas of cross section 1 mm2 are used. The wires are connected in series and one end of the combined wire is connected to a rigid support and other end is subjected to elongation. The stress required to produce a net elongation of 0.2 mm is,
[Given, the Young's Modulus for steel and brass are, respectively, 120×109 N/m2 and 60×109 N/m2 ]
question:
In an experiment, brass and steel wires of length 1 m each with areas of cross section 1 mm2 are used. The wires are connected in series and one end of the combined wire is connected to a rigid support and other end is subjected to elongation. The stress required to produce a net elongation of 0.2 mm is,
[Given, the Young's Modulus for steel and brass are, respectively, 120×109 N/m2 and 60×109 N/m2 ]
solution:
Stress will be same in both rods
Stress =Y1Δl1l=Y2Δl2l … (1)
And
Δl1+Δl2=0.2×10-3 m … (2)
From equation (1) and (2)
Stress ×lY1+lY2=0.2×10-3 m
Stress ×1120×109+160×109=0.2×10-3 m
Stress =8×106 N/m2

subject: Physics
q: If the ratio of lengths, radii and Young's moduli of steel and brass wires in the figure are $a, b$ and $c$ respectively, then the corresponding ratio of increase in their lengths is :
question: If the ratio of lengths, radii and Young's moduli of steel and brass wires in the figure are $a, b$ and $c$ respectively, then the corresponding ratio of increase in their lengths is :
solution:
According to questions,
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\ell_{\mathrm{b}}}=\mathrm{a}, \frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\mathrm{b}, \frac{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\mathrm{c}, \frac{\Delta \ell \mathrm{s}}{\Delta \ell_{\mathrm{b}}}=? \\
& \text { As, } \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{F} \ell}{\mathrm{A} \Delta \ell} \Rightarrow \Delta \ell=\frac{\mathrm{F} \ell}{\mathrm{Ay}} \\
& \Delta \ell_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{mg} \ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{s}}}\left[\because \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{s}}=(\mathrm{M}+2 \mathrm{M}) \mathrm{g}\right] \\
& \Delta \ell_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{Mg}\ell_{\mathrm{b}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{b}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{b}}}\left[\because \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}=2 \mathrm{Mg}\right]
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \frac{\Delta \ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\Delta \ell_{\mathrm{b}}}=\frac{\frac{3 \mathrm{Mg} \ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{s}}}}{\frac{2 \mathrm{Mg} \cdot \ell_{\mathrm{b}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{b}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{b}}}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{b}^2 \mathrm{C}}$

subject: Physics
q: The elastic potential energy stored in a steel wire of length 20 m stretched through 2 cm is 80 J. The cross sectional area of the wire is _____ mm2. (Given, Y=2.0×1011 N m–2)
question: The elastic potential energy stored in a steel wire of length 20 m stretched through 2 cm is 80 J. The cross sectional area of the wire is _____ mm2. (Given, Y=2.0×1011 N m–2)
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material is given by

Y=stressstrain=stress∆lL   ...1

The formula to calculate the potential energy U stored into the wire is given by

U=12×stress×strain×volume=12×stress×∆lL×A×L= 12×stress×A×∆l   ...2

From equation (1) and equation (2), it can be written that

U=12×Y×∆l2L×A   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the required cross-sectional area of the wire.

80 J=12×2.0×1011 Nm-2×0.02 m220 m×A⇒A= 2×80 J×202.0×1011×4×10-4 Nm-1=4×10-5 m2×106 mm21 m2= 40 mm2

subject: Physics
q: A cube has side length $5$ cm and modulus of rigidity $10^5$ N/m$^2$. The displacement produced by a force of $10$ N in the upper face of cube is _____ mm.
question: A cube has side length $5$ cm and modulus of rigidity $10^5$ N/m$^2$. The displacement produced by a force of $10$ N in the upper face of cube is _____ mm.
solution:
Given:
Side length of the cube, $L = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
Modulus of rigidity, $\eta = 10^5$ N/m$^2$
Force applied on the upper face, $F = 10$ N

Area of the upper face, $A = L^2 = (0.05)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m$^2$

Shear stress is given by:
$\text{Stress} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{10}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4000$ N/m$^2$

Shear strain is given by:
$\text{Strain} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L}$

Modulus of rigidity is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain:
$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \dfrac{F/A}{\Delta x/L}$

Rearranging for displacement $\Delta x$:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot \eta}$

Substituting the values:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{10 \times 0.05}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^5}$

$\Delta x = \dfrac{0.5}{250} = 0.002$ m

Converting to millimeters:
$\Delta x = 0.002 \times 1000 = 2$ mm

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q:
The fractional compression \(\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right)\) of water at the depth of 2.5 km below the sea level is
______ \(\%\). Given, the Bulk modulus of water \(=2 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}\), density of water \(=10^3\) \(\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}\), acceleration due to gravity \(=\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}\).
question:
The fractional compression \(\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right)\) of water at the depth of 2.5 km below the sea level is
______ \(\%\). Given, the Bulk modulus of water \(=2 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}\), density of water \(=10^3\) \(\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}\), acceleration due to gravity \(=\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}\).
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{gh}}{\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}}\right)} \\ & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}} \times 100=\frac{\rho \mathrm{gh}}{\mathrm{B}} \times 100 \\ & \frac{1000 \times 10 \times 2.5 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^9} \times 100 \% \\ & =1.25 \%\end{aligned}$
